4 min read
Just now

--

I spent the day fixing the unglamorous parts of GetPrompting. The lesson was simple: better content helps, but better paths help people actually use it.
Most SEO advice sounds exciting until you actually sit down to do the work.
Then it becomes a lot of small, unglamorous questions.
Does this page have a clear next step?
Do these articles link to each other in a way that helps a real reader?
Does the sitemap actually reflect what exists?
Are the product pages explaining the outcome before the setup details?
That is the kind of work I spent time doing on GetPrompting, and honestly, it reminded me why small sites often struggle even when the writing is decent.
The problem is not always content quality.
Sometimes the problem is that the site does not guide people anywhere.
More Content Does Not Automatically Mean More Clarity
When you have a newer site, it is tempting to keep writing.
Another article.
Another tutorial.
Another list of tools.
That can help, but only if the existing site has enough structure to support the new work. Otherwise, every article becomes its own little island. Google can see that the pages exist, but readers do not get a clear sense of what the site is actually trying to help them do.
That was one of the biggest lessons from the cleanup.
GetPrompting had useful material around prompts, local AI, n8n, SOPs, and workflow systems. But usefulness spread across many pages is not the same thing as a clear learning path.
So the question became:
What should someone do after they land here?
That one question exposed a lot.
Internal Links Should Feel Like Guidance, Not Decoration
Internal linking can get weird fast.
If you treat it like an SEO trick, you end up with paragraphs that feel like someone sprinkled blue text over them because a checklist said so.
That is not helpful.
A good internal link should feel like a natural next step.
If someone is reading about building an n8n workflow, it makes sense to point them toward a free workflow library or a related beginner guide. If someone is reading about prompt engineering, it makes sense to point them toward a practical prompt framework or SOP library.
What does not make sense is forcing every page to point to every product.
That might look busy, but it creates friction. The reader has to decode the site instead of learning from it.
So the cleanup focused on a simple rule:
Every link should answer a reader question.
Not “Where can I shove another keyword?”
Not “Can I make this page pass more authority?”
Just:
If I were reading this article, what would actually help me next?
Article Endings Matter More Than I Thought
One of the biggest leaks was at the end of articles.
A lot of people read, skim, or search their way to a single post. They may never touch the homepage. They may never see the navigation. They may not know there is a free starter pack, a workflow library, or a related guide.
If the article ends with nothing useful, the session ends there too.
That does not mean every article needs a giant sales pitch.
In fact, that is usually worse.
For GetPrompting, the cleaner answer was a simple newsletter and free starter pack block at the end of blog posts, paired with a natural way to keep reading. No giant product billboard. No “buy this now” energy. Just:
Here is a useful next step.
That is much closer to how the brand should feel.
Helpful first.
Commercial second.
Sitemaps and Schema Are Not Glamorous, But They Matter
Another reminder: technical SEO is easy to ignore until Search Console complains.
A stale sitemap can make your site look less organized than it really is. Missing or messy structured data can create unnecessary noise. Comments, duplicate signals, old CTAs, and outdated product framing can all send tiny mixed messages.
None of these fixes are magic.
But together, they reduce confusion.
Search engines do not just need pages. They need clear relationships between pages. Readers need the same thing.
That is where the boring work starts to matter.
The Real Goal Is Trust
The more I work on AI content, the more I think SEO is partly a trust exercise.
Yes, keywords matter.
Yes, titles matter.
Yes, backlinks matter.
But if someone lands on your site and cannot tell what you do, who the page is for, or where to go next, the traffic is not worth much.
For a small AI site, trust is built through practical consistency:

• Articles that solve specific problems

• Pages that connect naturally

• Free resources that actually help

• Product pages that explain outcomes clearly

• CTAs that respect the reader
That is the quiet work behind a better site.
It is not as exciting as launching a new product or publishing a giant guide.
But it is the work that makes everything else easier to understand.
My Takeaway
If you are building a small content site, do not only ask, “What should I publish next?”
Ask:
Can a new reader understand the site in one minute?
Can they find the next useful resource without guessing?
Do my links help them move forward?
Does every major page support the same promise?
That is the kind of cleanup I am trying to keep doing on GetPrompting. Not because it is flashy, but because the whole point is to help people build practical AI workflows without getting lost in tool clutter.
Sometimes growth starts with publishing more.
Sometimes it starts with making the thing you already built easier to trust.
